description: Realty Income is a large U.S.-based net lease REIT that owns diversified commercial properties leased primarily under long-term agreements to retail and service-oriented tenants.

overview.title: Business Overview
paragraphs: Realty Income is a real estate investment trust focused on owning freestanding commercial properties leased to tenants under long-term net lease agreements. The company is best known for its monthly dividend positioning, but the operating engine is straightforward: acquire and manage income-producing properties where tenants typically cover taxes, insurance, and maintenance.

Its portfolio is heavily exposed to retail and service categories, with tenants spread across industries intended to reduce reliance on any single chain, format, or consumer trend. In practice, the model depends less on technological novelty and more on tenant credit quality, lease duration, acquisition discipline, and the cost of capital, which is a refreshingly old-fashioned way to make spreadsheets feel heroic.

overview.title: Registry Relevance
paragraphs: For the Free The World registry, Realty Income maps most naturally to real estate infrastructure rather than a consumer technology platform. Its primary product should be represented as a net lease real estate portfolio, with any downstream analysis focused on how capital, property ownership, tenant concentration, and physical retail infrastructure shape market power.

No technology waves are currently mapped in repo context, and that appears appropriate for a first structured sync. Future research could still examine adjacent themes such as property data platforms, energy management, tenant analytics, or retail infrastructure dependencies, but those should be added only if supported by concrete company disclosures.

overview.title: Risk And Market Context
paragraphs: Realty Income’s business is sensitive to interest rates, acquisition spreads, tenant health, and broader demand for physical commercial locations. Because REITs distribute much of their income, access to debt and equity capital is central to growth; higher financing costs can make even very stable rent checks look less magical.

The company’s diversification across tenants and industries is a core strength, but it does not eliminate exposure to bankruptcies, store closures, lease renegotiations, or shifts in consumer behavior. The research profile should therefore avoid treating the portfolio as passive infrastructure and instead track the economic dependencies behind the rent stream.
